Math4111Introduction to Real Analysis (Lecture 20)

Lecture 20

Review

Using the binomial theorem, prove that

10!+11!+12!+⋯+1n!≥(1+1n)n\frac{1}{0!}+\frac{1}{1!}+\frac{1}{2!}+\cdots +\frac{1}{n!}\geq \left(1+\frac{1}{n}\right)^n0!1​+1!1​+2!1​+⋯+n!1​≥(1+n1​)n

Binomial theorem: (x+y)n=∑k=0n(nk)xn−kyk(x+y)^n = \sum_{k=0}^{n} \binom{n}{k} x^{n-k} y^k(x+y)n=∑k=0n​(kn​)xn−kyk (nk)=n!k!(n−k)!\binom{n}{k} = \frac{n!}{k!(n-k)!}(kn​)=k!(n−k)!n!​

Proof:

(1+1n)n=∑k=0n(nk)(1)n−k(1n)k=∑k=0n(nk)1nk=∑k=0n1k!∏j=1kn−j+1n\begin{aligned} \left(1+\frac{1}{n}\right)^n &= \sum_{k=0}^{n} \binom{n}{k} \left(1\right)^{n-k} \left(\frac{1}{n}\right)^k \\ &= \sum_{k=0}^{n} \binom{n}{k} \frac{1}{n^k} \\ &= \sum_{k=0}^{n} \frac{1}{k!} \prod_{j=1}^{k} \frac{n-j+1}{n} \\ \end{aligned}(1+n1​)n​=k=0∑n​(kn​)(1)n−k(n1​)k=k=0∑n​(kn​)nk1​=k=0∑n​k!1​j=1∏k​nn−j+1​​

Since j≥1j\geq 1j≥1, n−j+1n≤1\frac{n-j+1}{n} \leq1nn−j+1​≤1.

=∑k=0n1k!∏j=1kn−j+1n≥∑k=0n1k!\begin{aligned} &= \sum_{k=0}^{n} \frac{1}{k!} \prod_{j=1}^{k} \frac{n-j+1}{n} \\ &\geq \sum_{k=0}^{n} \frac{1}{k!} \\ \end{aligned}​=k=0∑n​k!1​j=1∏k​nn−j+1​≥k=0∑n​k!1​​

New material

Series

Definition 3.30

e=∑n=0∞1n!e=\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n!}e=n=0∑∞​n!1​

Lemma 3.30

∑n=0∞1n!\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n!}∑n=0∞​n!1​ converges.

Proof:

If n≥2n\geq 2n≥2,

1n!=1n⋅1(n−1)!≤12⋅12⋅⋯⋅12=12n−1\begin{aligned} \frac{1}{n!} &= \frac{1}{n} \cdot \frac{1}{(n-1)!} \\ &\leq \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \cdot \dots \cdot \frac{1}{2} \\ &= \frac{1}{2^{n-1}} \end{aligned}n!1​​=n1​⋅(n−1)!1​≤21​⋅21​⋅⋯⋅21​=2n−11​​ 1n!≤12n−1\frac{1}{n!} \leq \frac{1}{2^{n-1}}n!1​≤2n−11​

So ∑n=0∞1n!\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n!}∑n=0∞​n!1​ converges.

Theorem 3.31

lim⁡n→∞(1+1n)n=e\lim_{n\to\infty} \left(1+\frac{1}{n}\right)^n = en→∞lim​(1+n1​)n=e

Proof:

Let sn=∑k=0n1k!s_n = \sum_{k=0}^{n} \frac{1}{k!}sn​=∑k=0n​k!1​, let tn=(1+1n)nt_n = \left(1+\frac{1}{n}\right)^ntn​=(1+n1​)n.

Goal: lim⁡n→∞sn=lim⁡n→∞tn\lim_{n\to\infty} s_n = \lim_{n\to\infty} t_nlimn→∞​sn​=limn→∞​tn​. we already proved lim⁡n→∞sn\lim_{n\to\infty} s_nlimn→∞​sn​ exists. But we don’t know yet if lim⁡n→∞tn\lim_{n\to\infty} t_nlimn→∞​tn​ exists.

By warmup exercise, ∀n≥0,tn≤sn\forall n\geq 0, t_n \leq s_n∀n≥0,tn​≤sn​.

So if lim sup⁡n→∞tn≤lim sup⁡n→∞sn\limsup_{n\to\infty} t_n \leq \limsup_{n\to\infty} s_nlimsupn→∞​tn​≤limsupn→∞​sn​, then lim⁡n→∞tn\lim_{n\to\infty} t_nlimn→∞​tn​ exists and lim⁡n→∞tn=lim⁡n→∞sn\lim_{n\to\infty} t_n = \lim_{n\to\infty} s_nlimn→∞​tn​=limn→∞​sn​.

Now we will show lim sup⁡n→∞tn≥e\limsup_{n\to\infty} t_n \geq elimsupn→∞​tn​≥e.

Ideas: (special case of the argument)

If n≥2n\geq 2n≥2, then

tn=∑k=0n(nk)(1n)k≥(n0)+(n1)1n+(n2)(1n)2+⋯+(nn)(1n)n=1+nn+n(n−1)2n2+⋯+1nn\begin{aligned} t_n &= \sum_{k=0}^{n} \binom{n}{k} \left(\frac{1}{n}\right)^k \\ &\geq \binom{n}{0} + \binom{n}{1}\frac{1}{n} + \binom{n}{2}\left(\frac{1}{n}\right)^2 + \cdots + \binom{n}{n}\left(\frac{1}{n}\right)^n \\ &= 1 + \frac{n}{n} + \frac{n(n-1)}{2n^2} + \cdots + \frac{1}{n^n} \\ \end{aligned}tn​​=k=0∑n​(kn​)(n1​)k≥(0n​)+(1n​)n1​+(2n​)(n1​)2+⋯+(nn​)(n1​)n=1+nn​+2n2n(n−1)​+⋯+nn1​​

Let n→∞n\to\inftyn→∞, then

lim inf⁡n→∞tn≥1+1+12+13+⋯\liminf_{n\to\infty} t_n \geq 1 + 1 + \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{3} + \cdotsn→∞liminf​tn​≥1+1+21​+31​+⋯

Fix m≥2m\geq 2m≥2, for any n≥mn\geq mn≥m,

tn≥10!+11!+12!nnn−1n⋯+1m!nnn−1n⋯n−m+1nt_n \geq \frac{1}{0!} + \frac{1}{1!} + \frac{1}{2!}\frac{n}{n}\frac{n-1}{n}\cdots+\frac{1}{m!}\frac{n}{n}\frac{n-1}{n}\cdots\frac{n-m+1}{n}tn​≥0!1​+1!1​+2!1​nn​nn−1​⋯+m!1​nn​nn−1​⋯nn−m+1​

Let n→∞n\to\inftyn→∞, then

lim inf⁡n→∞tn≥10!+11!+12!+⋯+1m!=sm\liminf_{n\to\infty} t_n \geq \frac{1}{0!} + \frac{1}{1!} + \frac{1}{2!} + \cdots + \frac{1}{m!}=s_mn→∞liminf​tn​≥0!1​+1!1​+2!1​+⋯+m!1​=sm​

So lim inf⁡n→∞tn≥lim⁡n→∞sn=e\liminf_{n\to\infty} t_n \geq \lim_{n\to\infty} s_n = eliminfn→∞​tn​≥limn→∞​sn​=e.

Therefore, e≤lim inf⁡n→∞tn≤lim sup⁡n→∞tn≤ee\leq \liminf_{n\to\infty} t_n\leq \limsup_{n\to\infty} t_n\leq ee≤liminfn→∞​tn​≤limsupn→∞​tn​≤e.

So lim⁡n→∞tn\lim_{n\to\infty} t_nlimn→∞​tn​ exists and lim⁡n→∞tn=e\lim_{n\to\infty} t_n = elimn→∞​tn​=e.

QED

Theorem 3.32

eee is irrational.

Q: How good is the approximation is sns_nsn​ to eee?

A: Very good actually.

e−sn=∑k=n+1∞1k!<1(n+1)!(1+1n+1+1(n+1)2+⋯ )=1(n+1)!∑k=0∞(1n+1)k=1(n+1)!11−1n+1=1n!⋅1n<1n!n\begin{aligned} e-s_n &= \sum_{k=n+1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{k!} \\ &<\frac{1}{(n+1)!}\left(1+\frac{1}{n+1}+\frac{1}{(n+1)^2}+\cdots\right) \\ &=\frac{1}{(n+1)!}\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\left(\frac{1}{n+1}\right)^k \\ &=\frac{1}{(n+1)!}\frac{1}{1-\frac{1}{n+1}} \\ &=\frac{1}{n!}\cdot\frac{1}{n} \\ &<\frac{1}{n!n} \end{aligned}e−sn​​=k=n+1∑∞​k!1​<(n+1)!1​(1+n+11​+(n+1)21​+⋯)=(n+1)!1​k=0∑∞​(n+11​)k=(n+1)!1​1−n+11​1​=n!1​⋅n1​<n!n1​​

Proof:

Suppose e=pqe=\frac{p}{q}e=qp​ for some p,q∈Np,q\in\mathbb{N}p,q∈N.

Observe that:

sq=1+1+12+⋯+1q!s_q=1+1+\frac{1}{2}+\cdots+\frac{1}{q!}sq​=1+1+21​+⋯+q!1​

So q!sqq! s_qq!sq​ is an integer.

Since e=pqe=\frac{p}{q}e=qp​, q!eq!eq!e is an integer, q!(e−sq)q!(e-s_q)q!(e−sq​) is an integer.

However,

0<q!(e−sq)<q!q!q<1q0<q!(e-s_q)<\frac{q!}{q!q}<\frac{1}{q}0<q!(e−sq​)<q!qq!​<q1​

Contradiction.

QED

The root and ratio tests

This is a fancy way of using comparison test with geometric series.

Theorem 3.33 (Root test)

∣an∣n≤α  ⟹  ∣an∣≤αn \sqrt[n]{|a_n|} \leq \alpha \implies |a_n|\leq \alpha^nn∣an​∣​≤α⟹∣an​∣≤αn

Given a series ∑n=0∞an\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n∑n=0∞​an​, put α=lim sup⁡n→∞∣an∣n\alpha = \limsup_{n\to\infty} \sqrt[n]{|a_n|}α=limsupn→∞​n∣an​∣​.

Then

(a) If α<1\alpha < 1α<1, then ∑n=0∞an\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n∑n=0∞​an​ converges.
(b) If α>1\alpha > 1α>1, then ∑n=0∞an\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n∑n=0∞​an​ diverges.
(c) If α=1\alpha = 1α=1, the test gives no information

Proof:

(a) Suppose α<1\alpha < 1α<1. Then ∃β\exists \beta∃β such that α<β<1\alpha < \beta < 1α<β<1.

By Theorem 3.17(b), ∀n≥N,∣an∣n<β\forall n\geq N, \sqrt[n]{|a_n|} < \beta∀n≥N,n∣an​∣​<β.

So ∀n≥N,∣an∣<βn\forall n\geq N, |a_n| < \beta^n∀n≥N,∣an​∣<βn.

By comparison test, ∑n=0∞an\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n∑n=0∞​an​ converges.

(b) Suppose α>1\alpha > 1α>1. By Theorem 3.17(a), {n∈N:∣an∣n>1}\{n\in \mathbb{N}: \sqrt[n]{|a_n|} > 1\}{n∈N:n∣an​∣​>1} is infinite.

Thus an↛0a_n\not\to 0an​→0, ∑n=0∞an\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n∑n=0∞​an​ diverges.

(c) ∑n=0∞1n\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n}∑n=0∞​n1​ and ∑n=0∞1n2\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n^2}∑n=0∞​n21​ both have α=1\alpha = 1α=1. but the first diverges and the second converges.

QED

Theorem 3.34 (Ratio test)

∣an+1an∣≤α  ⟹  ∣an∣≤αn \left|\frac{a_{n+1}}{a_n}\right| \leq \alpha \implies |a_n|\leq \alpha^n​an​an+1​​​≤α⟹∣an​∣≤αn

Given a series ∑n=0∞an\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n∑n=0∞​an​, an∈C\{0}a_n\in\mathbb{C}\backslash\{0\}an​∈C\{0}.

Then

(a) If lim sup⁡n→∞∣an+1an∣<1\limsup_{n\to\infty} \left|\frac{a_{n+1}}{a_n}\right| < 1limsupn→∞​​an​an+1​​​<1, then ∑n=0∞an\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n∑n=0∞​an​ converges.
(b) If ∣an+1an∣≥1\left|\frac{a_{n+1}}{a_n}\right| \geq 1​an​an+1​​​≥1 for all n≥n0n\geq n_0n≥n0​ for some n0∈Nn_0\in\mathbb{N}n0​∈N, then ∑n=0∞an\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n∑n=0∞​an​ diverges.

Remark:

  1. If lim sup⁡n→∞∣an+1an∣=1\limsup_{n\to\infty} \left|\frac{a_{n+1}}{a_n}\right| = 1limsupn→∞​​an​an+1​​​=1, the test gives no information.
  2. If lim sup⁡n→∞∣an+1an∣>1\limsup_{n\to\infty} \left|\frac{a_{n+1}}{a_n}\right| > 1limsupn→∞​​an​an+1​​​>1, the test gives no information.

Proof:

(b) ∀n≥n0,∣an+1an∣≥1\forall n\geq n_0, \left|\frac{a_{n+1}}{a_n}\right| \geq 1∀n≥n0​,​an​an+1​​​≥1.

So an0↛0a_{n_0}\not\to 0an0​​→0, ∑n=0∞an\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n∑n=0∞​an​ diverges.

(a) β∈(lim sup⁡n→∞∣an+1an∣,1)\beta \in(\limsup_{n\to\infty} \left|\frac{a_{n+1}}{a_n}\right|, 1)β∈(limsupn→∞​​an​an+1​​​,1).

By Theorem 3.17(b), ∃N\exists N∃N such that ∀n≥N,∣an+1an∣<β<1\forall n\geq N, \left|\frac{a_{n+1}}{a_n}\right| < \beta < 1∀n≥N,​an​an+1​​​<β<1.

So,

∣aN∣<β∣aN∣∣aN+1∣<β∣aN+1∣∣aN+2∣<β∣aN+2∣\begin{aligned} |a_N| &< \beta|a_N|\\ |a_{N+1}| &< \beta|a_{N+1}|\\ |a_{N+2}| &< \beta|a_{N+2}|\\ \end{aligned}∣aN​∣∣aN+1​∣∣aN+2​∣​<β∣aN​∣<β∣aN+1​∣<β∣aN+2​∣​

i.e. ∀n≥N,∣an∣<βn−N∣aN∣=βn(β−N∣aN∣)\forall n\geq N, |a_n| < \beta^{n-N}|a_N|=\beta^n(\beta^{-N}|a_N|)∀n≥N,∣an​∣<βn−N∣aN​∣=βn(β−N∣aN​∣).

Since ∑n=N∞βn\sum_{n=N}^{\infty} \beta^n∑n=N∞​βn converges, by comparison test, ∑n=0∞an\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n∑n=0∞​an​ converges.

QED

We will skip Theorem 3.37. One implication is that if ratio test can be applied, then root test can be applied.

Power series

Definition 3.38

Let (cn)(c_n)(cn​) be a sequence of complex numbers. A power series is a series of the form

∑n=0∞cnzn\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} c_n z^nn=0∑∞​cn​zn

Theorem 3.39

Given a power series ∑n=0∞cnzn\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} c_n z^n∑n=0∞​cn​zn, let R=1lim sup⁡n→∞∣cn∣nR=\frac{1}{\limsup_{n\to\infty} \sqrt[n]{|c_n|}}R=limsupn→∞​n∣cn​∣​1​.

Then

(a) The series converges absolutely for all z∈Cz\in\mathbb{C}z∈C with ∣z∣<R|z| < R∣z∣<R.
(b) The series diverges for all z∈Cz\in\mathbb{C}z∈C with ∣z∣>R|z| > R∣z∣>R.
(c) If 0≤r<R0\leq r < R0≤r<R, then the series converges uniformly on the closed disk {z∈C:∣z∣≤r}\{z\in\mathbb{C}: |z|\leq r\}{z∈C:∣z∣≤r}.

Proof:

lim sup⁡n→∞∣cnzn∣n=lim sup⁡n→∞∣cn∣n⋅∣z∣=∣z∣R\begin{aligned} \limsup_{n\to\infty} \sqrt[n]{|c_n z^n|} &= \limsup_{n\to\infty} \sqrt[n]{|c_n|} \cdot |z| \\ &= \frac{|z|}{R} \end{aligned}n→∞limsup​n∣cn​zn∣​​=n→∞limsup​n∣cn​∣​⋅∣z∣=R∣z∣​​

By root test, the series converges absolutely for all z∈Cz\in\mathbb{C}z∈C with ∣z∣<R|z| < R∣z∣<R.

QED
